title: 第 45 節
en: No doubt, etymology is an immense help, and a successful comparison of mythological names in Sanskrit, Zend, Greek, and Latin serves sometimes as an important indication, sometimes as an encouraging confirmation of our views on the character of mythology and on the origin of its gods and heroes. But the fundamental principles of Comparative Mythology must stand or fall by themselves.

Suppose we were doubtful as to the origin of certain ecclesiastical vestments used in Tibet which are very like those used at Eome, would not the whole controversy be settled at once, if we found any such names as dalmatic, cope, chasuble in Tibetan ? This shows the value of names. Still if we knew nothing of etymology, nothing of a comparison of mythological names in Sanskrit, Greek, and Latin, the acts which in the Veda are ascribed to Agni would leave no doubt in our mind that he was in Vedic India the god of fire, recognised not only in the fire on the hearth, and in the flames of the sacrifice, but likewise in the flash of lightning, in the gleam of the dawn, in the fierce light of the midday sun, and in the glory of the sunset. We do not want etymo- A a 2 782 AGNI. [CHAP.
logy or comparisons to tell us who Selene or Artemis was, who was Phoibos or even Phoibos Apollon.

The Vedic Ushas, if we knew nothing of the meaning of the word and its derivation from the root vas, to shine, is so clearly painted by the ancient Hishis, that no one would hesitate for one moment to recognise in her a representative of the Dawn in its various aspects. And if Ushas is once recognised as the Dawn, many things follow by themselves. Her lover must surely stand for some heavenly phenomenon in close connection with her. If the light that follows the Dawn is called Helios or Hyperion, we want but little knowledge of Greek to see that Hyperion like Helios is meant for a solar being. If the Vedic Indra were etymologically unconnected with the root which appears in ind-u, rain-drop, any hymn in the Veda would tell us that he was conceived as the agent in the sky who gathered the clouds (vecpeXrjyepeTa), who slew the nebular demons in the dark thunderstorm, and sent the rain-drops down on the parched earth. And if we know this, we should also know that Zeus in his character of cloud-gatherer sprang from the same source as Indra, though there is no trace of that name in Greek or Latin.

When Darmesteter proved that Ahura Mazda was the same god as Vanma in the Veda, he did not undertake to show that the names were the same or even derived from the same root. But he showed that the deeds and the characters of the two gods were the same, so much so that when we have in the Veda the divine couple Mitra-Varuna, we have corresponding to it in the Avesta the divine couple of Mithra and Ahura.

Vl] AGNI. 783 Considering how peculiarly Asura was a name of Varuna in the Veda, are we to suppose that there was no historical connection between the compound Mitra-Vanmau in the Veda and Mithra- Asura in the Avesta ?

While the name of Indra cannot be matched in the language and legends of any of the NorthWestern Aryas, Agni, though unknown as the name of a deity anywhere but in India, is well known as a name of fire in Latin and the Slavonic languages. This is one of the great advantages which Sanskrit, and more particularly Vedic - Sanskrit, offers for mythological studies. Whereas in Greek and Latin the names of gods have nearly all lost their appellative meaning, and oppose great difficulties to an etymological analysis, we find that many of the Vedic names have retained their appellative power in the later language, or admit at all events of etymological analysis. Thus Agni means fire in Sanskrit to the present day.

Dyaus, though no longer used as a masculine, means sky as a feminine in post -Vedic as well as in Vedic-Sanskrit. PWthivi continues to mean earth, anila, wind, ap, water, aranyam, forest, aryaman, sun, aditya, sun, indu, moon, indraMpa, rainbow, ie/a, earth, ushas, dawn, ritu, month, ^atavedas, fire, tanunapat, fire, tvashiW, carpenter, dhatri, maker, nirWti, destruction, pathya, beneficial, pan/anya, rain-cloud, pitara/i, fathers, pushan, sun, prism, ray of light, bhumi, earth, marut, wind, mitra, sun, yama, twin, death, ratri, night, rodasi, heaven and earth, vanaspati, tree, varu?ia, ocean, vayu, wind, vaisvanara, fire, sarariyu, wind, cloud, savitW, sun, stirya, sun, soma, moon, and several more. It would 784 AGNI. [chap.

be impossible to produce a similar list of intelligible names from among the gods of Greek or Roman mythology. And though it is quite true that the more recent meanings of some of these names are different from their Vedic meanings and may actually prove misleading, yet in most cases they indicate very clearly the original character of Yedic deities. In cases therefore where the later meaning diners from the Vedic, it becomes our duty to try to discover the links which connect the two ; nay even in cases where such links cannot be found, we may rest convinced that they once existed.
Agni in India and Persia.

No doubt, the name of Agni existed before the Aryan Separation, yet there is no trace anywhere except in the Veda of Agni as the name of a deity. We have in Latin the same word, ignis, and in Old Slav, ogn'1, in Lituanian ugnis, showing that the word was known to several branches of the Aryan family, the Italian, the Slavonic, nay possibly the Teutonic also, provided that Scottish ingle comes really from the same source. But we find no trace anywhere, not even in Persia, of fire being worshipped or deified under the name of Agni.

In Greece fire received divine honours under the name of Hestia, in Rome under that of Vesta. We know that in other parts of Europe also fire was worshipped before and after the introduction of Christianity, but we do not know the names of the 1 The father and head of a family is still called in Kussian ogniscaninu, ognistaninu, the arranger and keeper of the fire on the hearth, the fire-priest. See Krek, 1. c, p. 203.
Vi] AGNI IN INDIA AND PERSIA. 785 god of fire. I quote from Mannhardt, Lett. Sonn., p. 290 : —

'Dtugoss, in describing the conversion of Upper Lituania, writes : " Ignis qui per sacerdotes subject is lignis nocte atque interdiu colebatur." Of Witold, when suppressing heathen practices in Samagithia, it is said: "Et ad praecipuum Samagitharum numen, ignem videlicet, quern sacrosanctum et perpetuum putabant, qui in montis altissimi jugo super nuvium Nyewasza sito lignorum assidua appositione a sacrorum sacerdote alebatur, accedens turrim, in qua consistebat, incendit et ignem disjicit et extinguit." Hieronymus, who came as a missionary from Prague to Samagithia : " Post hoc gentem reperit, quae sacrum colebat ignem eumque perpetuum appellabant. Sacerdotes semper materiam, ne deficeret, ministrabant." :
The Five Agnis in India and Persia.

But while the name of Agni is absent in the Avesta, several of his epithets have been preserved in it, such Apam napa£, son of the water, corresponding to the Apam napai of the Veda, the light arising from and setting in the waters of the sea or of the sky ».

Every one of the fires mentioned in the Veda can be matched in the Avesta. The vohufryana, the fire or warmth of the body, is matched by the Sanskrit gkth&ra,, fire; the urvazista, the fire in the plants, by the Sk. aushadha ; the berezisavanh, living in the earth, by the fire in the stones (asmasu) ; the vazista, the fire of the lightning, by the Sk. vaidyuta. Thus we read in the Atharva-veda III, 21, 1 : ' Let this offering be for the fires which are 1 Rv. Ill, 25, 5: Agne apam sam idhyase durowe, 'Agni, thou art kindled in the house of the waters. '
786 THE FIVE AGNIS IN INDIA AND PERSIA. [chap.
in the waters, in the cloud, in man, in stones, and for those which have entered the plants and the trees.'

These five fires represent probably a secondary and rather systematising stage in the mythological conception of Agni, still they must have been fixed upon before the Indo-Iranian Separation. The fire in the waters seems to be meant for the solar light which sets in the clouds or in the sea, and appears again every morning, after passing the night in the waters ; the fire in the cloud is the lightning, the fire in man the vital warmth or digestive heat ; the fire in the stones refers to the fire hidden in flints, while that in plants and trees is most likely meant for the fire supposed to be hidden in the wood, before it is brought out by friction, or possibly for the fire kept up by fire-wood and by dry leaves piled up around the first spark that has been obtained. I doubt whether, as has been supposed by some scholars, the mere sap or life of plants could have been called the fire in the plants.
Agni in the Veda.

Thus we see that while in Greek and Latin such names as Hephaistos, Vulcanus, Hestia, and Vesta are mere names with hardly any appellative power or etymological background, Agni in the Veda is simply a name of the fire, such as it was seen in every house and on every hearth. In some hymns we find Agni spoken of just as the ordinary fire would be. ' The priests,' we read, ' have by rubbing produced fire (agni) from the two fire-sticks. Agni rests in the double wood, as in his mother.' ' Agni is kindled by Agni,' &c. But how easily the fire on Vl] AGNI IN THE VEDA. 787 the hearth becomes something human and more than human, we can see elsewhere also, for instance, in the words addressed by a bride when she leaves her home, and says good-bye to the fire burning on her hearth. In the ' Sudauer Biichlein,' written between 1 526-1 530, giving a description of the NorthWestern corner of Samland, the bride in addressing the fire says : ' 0 my dear sacred little fire, Oho mey mile swente panike.' In the seventeenth century the Lituanians still called the fire szwenta Ponyke (ugnele), ' Sacred little fire.' And in the evening, when covering it up, they said : ' Sacred little fire, I shall bury thee very nicely that thou be not angry with me.' By such expressions the visible fire was imperceptibly changed into something personal, if not yet divine.

The fire might be called by many different names, and we can see in the Veda how the slightest change in the names addressed to Agni imparts to him a new active personality, a human, and even a superhuman character. He is called a messenger (I, 12, i), a master of the house or of the clan (I, 12, 2). He destroys evil spirits (I, 12, 5), and protects his friends (I, 12, 8), he is the providence (pramati) of the singer (I, 31, 9). The gods made him lord of men (I, 31, 11); he is the friend of the gods (I, 31, 1), he is like a father to his worshippers, they are his kinsfolk (I, 31, 10). No man, no god is greater than Agni (I, 19, 2), he is immortal (I, 13, 5), and brings men to the highest immortality (I, 31, 7); he is the first maker (I, 13, 10), i.e. the carpenter (tvash^ar) of the world. All this is intelligible even to us. When the fire on the altar carries offerings to the gods in the form of smoke rising to the sky, 788 AGNI IN THE VEDA. [chap.

Agni is naturally called the messenger. When he burns on the hearth, the centre of the house, he is the lord of the family, and when he drives away darkness and purifies the air, he is the destroyer of evil spirits. As life in a severe winter would be almost impossible without fire, he is the protector of those who keep him, he protects even the poor who take refuge with him. As sacrifice is impossible without Agni, he may well be called the friend of the gods, and as he is never allowed to go out, or as he always comes to life again, he is called immortal. Sometimes it is said that Agni is kindled by Agni (I, 12, 6), that is, that he produces himself, fire produces fire, and it is in this sense that he was called Tanunapat, offspring of his own body or selfborn. As his light in the morning reveals heaven and earth, he may be said to have manifested or produced heaven and earth, and having once been conceived as a maker or creator, we can well understand (1,67, 3) that he should have been represented as supporting the earth and as upholding the sky also by strong spells. This cosmogonic character of Agni has been so fully worked out by M. Senart \ that no more need be said about it. He has clearly shown how the morning was taken as the type of creation, and as Agni, the light, was every day the revealer 2 or maker of the world, how he was likewise conceived as the original creator in the beginning of time 3. Thus Agni, the light manifested in the 1 Senart, Legende du Buddha, p. 96.
2 Rv. IV, 3, 11 : Avi7j sva/i abhavat gate agnau, 'The skywas revealed when Agni had been born.'
3 Rv. VI, 7, 6 : Vaisvanarasya vimitani Mkshasa sanuni diva/e, ' The heights of heaven were created by his light.'

Vl] AGNI IN THE VEDA. 789 sun, was, like the sun, raised to the rank of a supreme deity, and every name assigned to Agni, all the divine characters in which Agni had become personified, whether SavitW, Vishmi, Vu%, Rohita, Skambha, Pra</apati, and still later Purusha and Brahman, were raised to the same rank.

We must also remember that a belief in the post hoc, propter hoc, is at the root of many ancient myths, and that in this way people brought themselves to believe, or, at least, to say, that the morning sacrifice caused Agni or the sun to rise. Thus we read, Rv.V, 6, 4: ' We kindle thee, 0 Agni, brilliant, O god, and undying, so that thy more glorious torch may shine in the sky,' and this idea x was more fully carried out in the Brahmarias. Thus we read, £at.-brah. II, 3, 1-5 : 'And when he offers in the morning before sunrise, he produces that (sunchild), and, having become a light, it rises shining.'

After a time Agni, though he retains his name, is no longer merely the visible fire on the altar. As soon as his chief attributes, light and warmth, had been recognised in other parts of Nature, Agni was supposed to be present everywhere. The fire that was lighted on the hearth, and was implored not to go away, was in the same verse asked to shine by day or in the sky so that men might long see the sun, i. e. might live. Ath.-veda XII, 2,18: Samiddho agna ahuta sa no mabhyapakrami/i, atraiva didihi dyavi, <7y6k h& suryam drise. Thus Agni was perceived in the sun, and he was supposed to have made the sun, whether rising or setting. Rv. X, 156, 4: 'Agni, thou hast lifted to the sky that unfading star, 1 Cf. Bergaigne, i, p. 141 ; Oldenberg, Religion des Veda, p. no.
790 AGNI IN THE VEDA. [chap.
zh: 無庸置疑，語源學提供了巨大的幫助，將梵文、祆教語、希臘文及拉丁文中的神話名字進行成功的比較，有時可作為重要的指示，有時則作為鼓舞人心的確認，證實我們對神話特質及其諸神與英雄起源的看法。然而比較神話學的根本原則必須自成一體而自立。

假設我們對於西藏所使用的某些教會聖衣的來源感到懷疑，這些聖衣與羅馬所使用的非常相似，如果我們在藏文中發現了諸如 dalmatic、cope、chasuble 這樣的名稱，是否整個爭論就會立刻得到解決呢？這說明了名稱的價值。然而，即使我們對詞源學一無所知，對於梵文、希臘文和拉丁文中神話名稱的比較一無所知，吠陀中歸於阿格尼（Agni）的那些行為也會毫無疑問地讓我們確信，他在吠陀時代的印度乃是火神，被人們認知不僅在爐火之中，在祭祀的火焰之中，而且在閃電的閃光之中，在黎明的光輝之中，在正午烈日的熾烈光芒之中，以及在夕陽的榮光之中。我們不需要詞源學來
神話學或比較研究無法告訴我們賽琳娜（Selene）或阿爾忒彌斯是誰，福伊波斯（Phoibos）甚至福伊波斯‧阿波羅（Phoibos Apollon）是誰。

吠陀的烏莎（Ushas），即使我們對該詞的意義與其源自根詞 vas（閃耀）的語源毫無所知，古代的仙人（Rishi）已將她描繪得如此清晰，以至於沒有人會有片刻的遲疑，無法在她身上認出曙光各種樣態的代表。而一旦烏莎被認可為曙光，許多事物便自然而然地接踵而至。她的情人必然代表某個與她密切相關的天體現象。倘若追隨曙光而來的光被稱為赫利俄斯（Helios）或許珀律翁（Hyperion），我們只需略具希臘文知識，便能看出許珀律翁如赫利俄斯一般乃是意指太陽神。若吠陀的因陀羅（Indra）在詞源學上與出現於 ind-u（雨滴）中的根詞無關，吠陀中的任何一首讚歌都會告訴我們，他被構想為天空中的施事者，聚集雲層（vecpeXrjyepeTa）、在黑暗的雷雨中誅殺霧氣化身的惡魔，並將雨滴灑向乾涸的大地。而一旦我們知曉這一點，我們也應當知道，宙斯在其雲聚者的角色中源自與因陀羅相同的來源，儘管希臘文或拉丁文中完全沒有留下那個名字的痕跡。
當達爾梅斯特特證明了阿胡拉‧馬茲達是吠陀中的伐樓那同一位神祇時，他並未著手證明這兩個名字相同，甚至也未證明它們源出同一個語根。但他卻證明了這兩位神祇的事蹟與性格是相同的，以至於當我們在吠陀中發現神聖的配偶對密特拉‧伐樓那（Mitra-Varuna）時，在阿維斯陀中我們便發現了對應的米特拉與阿胡拉的神聖配偶對。
考慮到「阿蘇羅」（Asura）在《吠陀》中是瓦魯那（Varuna）的獨特名號，我們是否應該假定《吠陀》中的複合名「彌多羅‧瓦魯那」（Mitra-Varuna）與《阿維斯塔》中的「彌特拉‧阿蘇羅」（Mithra-Asura）之間毫無歷史淵源可言？

雖然因陀羅（Indra）這個名字在西北雅利安民族的任何語言和傳說中都無法找到對應詞，但火神阿耆尼（Agni）儘管作為神祇的名字僅在印度為人所知，卻在拉丁文和斯拉夫語言中是火的常見名稱。這是梵文，特別是吠陀梵文，為神話學研究所提供的眾多優勢之一。而在希臘文和拉丁文中，諸神的名字幾乎全已喪失其本義，對語源學分析構成巨大困難，相反地我們發現許多吠陀名字在後來的語言中仍保有其本義，或至少允許進行語源學分析。因此，阿耆尼（Agni）一詞在梵文中至今仍表示火。

特尤斯（Dyaus）雖然在後吠陀時期已不再作為陽性使用，但在吠陀梵文及後吠陀梵文中都表示「天空」（陰性）。普里西維（Prithivi）繼續表示地球，阿尼拉（anila）表示風，阿普（ap）表示水，阿蘭亞（aranyam）表示森林，阿爾耶曼（aryaman）表示太陽，阿迪提亞（aditya）表示太陽，因度（indu）表示月亮，因德拉帕（indra-pa）表示彩虹，伊拉（iela）表示地球，烏莎斯（ushas）表示曙光，律圖（ritu）表示月份，阿塔維達斯（atharva-vedas）表示火，塔努納帕特（tanunapat）表示火，特瓦什特里（tvashti）表示木匠，達特里（dhatri）表示製造者，尼里提（nirrti）表示毀滅，帕提亞（pathya）表示有益之物，帕尼亞（panjanya）表示雨雲，皮塔羅（pitara-i）表示祖先，普善（pushan）表示太陽、棱柱、光線，跋明（bhumi）表示地球，馬魯特（marut）表示風，密多羅（mitra）表示太陽，閻摩（yama）表示孿生者、死亡，拉特里（ratri）表示夜晚，羅達西（rodasi）表示天地，萬那斯帕提（vanaspati）表示樹木，伐樓那（varu?ia）表示海洋，瓦由（vayu）表示風，毘濕瓦那羅（vaisvanara）表示火，薩罹留（sarariyu）表示風、雲，薩維特里（savitri）表示太陽，蘇里耶（surya）表示太陽，蘇摩（soma）表示月亮，以及其他若干更多者。這樣列舉還不足以… [784 阿耆尼。[第…章
不可能從希臘或羅馬神話的諸神中編製出類似的可理解名稱清單。雖然這些名稱中某些較晚近的含義確實與吠陀的含義不同，且實際上可能導致誤導，然而在大多數情況下，它們清晰地指示了吠陀神祇的原始性格。因此，在後來的含義不同於吠陀含義的情況下，我們的職責就是試圖發現連結這兩者的紐帶；甚至在無法找到此類紐帶的情況下，我們也可以確信它們曾經存在過。
印度與波斯的阿耆尼

毫無疑問，Agni 這個名字在雅利安人分離之前就已存在，然而除了吠陀之外，我們在任何地方都找不到 Agni 作為一位提婆之名的痕跡。我們在拉丁文中有同一個詞彙 ignis，在古斯拉夫文中有 ogn'1，在立陶宛文中有 ugnis，這表明這個詞彙在雅利安家族的多個分支中都為人所知，包括義大利語族、斯拉夫語族，甚至可能還有條頓語族，只要蘇格蘭語的 ingle 確實來自同一來源的話。然而我們在任何地方都找不到任何痕跡，即使在波斯也不見其蹤影，表明火焰曾被崇拜或奉為神祇而稱為 Agni。
在希臘，火受到尊崇，以赫斯提亞（Hestia）之名被奉為神祇；在羅馬，則以灶神維斯塔（Vesta）之名受祭祀。我們知道歐洲其他地區的火崇拜在基督教傳入前後也曾存在，但我們不知曉這些地方火神的名稱。¹ ¹ 在俄羅斯，家族的父親與首領至今仍被稱為 ogniscaninu、ognistaninu，意為爐火的安排者與守護者、火祭司。參見 Krek，同上，第 203 頁。
[VI] 印度與波斯的阿格尼。785 火神。我引述曼哈特《萊特‧松》第290頁：——

杜戈什（Dtugoss）在描述立陶宛上地區的改宗時寫道：「由祭司們供奉的火焰，晝夜不息地以木柴燃燒著。」（Ignis qui per sacerdotes subject is lignis nocte atque interdiu colebatur.）關於維陶塔斯在薩莫吉提亞壓制異教風俗時，記載稱：「他來到薩莫吉提亞人的至高神祇──即火焰──之前，他們視之為神聖而永恆，這聖火在尼瓦紮山（Nyewasza）的最高山脊上由聖祭司不間斷地添置木柴而維持，他進入了火焰所在的塔樓，點燃、驅散並熄滅了它。」（Et ad praecipuum Samagitharum numen, ignem videlicet, quern sacrosanctum et perpetuum putabant, qui in montis altissimi jugo super nuvium Nyewasza sito lignorum assidua appositione a sacrorum sacerdote alebatur, accedens turrim, in qua consistebat, incendit et ignem disjicit et extinguit.）耶羅尼姆斯從布拉格來到薩莫吉提亞任傳教士時寫道：「其後他發現一個民族，他們供奉聖火，並稱之為永恆之火。祭司們始終供應燃料，以使其不熄滅。」（Post hoc gentem reperit, quae sacrum colebat ignem eumque perpetuum appellabant. Sacerdotes semper materiam, ne deficeret, ministrabant.）
印度與波斯的五位阿格尼
然而，儘管阿格尼（Agni）的名號在《阿維斯陀》中未見蹤跡，但他的若干尊稱卻保存於其中，例如「水之子」（Apam napai）——對應於吠陀中的「Apam napai」——乃是自海水或天空之水中升起並沒入其中的光明。

吠陀中提及的每一種火都可以在阿維斯陀中找到相應的對照。**vohufryana**（身體的火或溫熱）與梵文 *gharti*（火）相對應；**urvazista**（植物中的火）與梵文 *aushadha*（草藥）相對應；**berezisavanh**（居於地中）與石頭中的火（*asmasu*）相對應；**vazista**（閃電之火）與梵文 *vaidyuta*（電）相對應。因此我們在《雅闍吠陀》第三卷第二十一章第一節讀到：「願此供物獻與諸火，」¹ --- ¹ *Rv.* III, 25, 5：Agne apam sam idhyase durowe，「阿耆尼呵，汝於水之家中被燃燒。」
786 印度與波斯的五位阿格尼。[章
在水中、在雲中、在人身上、在石頭中，以及那些進入植物和樹木的。」

這五種火焰可能代表了阿格尼（Agni）神話觀念的次級階段，且帶有相當程度的體系化特徵，然而它們必定在印度─伊朗分離之前就已確立。水中之火似乎是指落入雲層或海中的太陽光，每早晨在經歷夜間於水中的運行後再次出現；雲中之火乃閃電，人體中之火乃生命的溫熱或消化熱；石頭中之火指隱藏於燧石中的火，而植物和樹木中之火很可能是指據稱隱藏於木材中的火，在未透過摩擦將其引出之前，或者可能指由木柴和乾葉堆積在第一粒火星周圍所維持的火。我懷疑某些學者所推測的說法，即植物中的單純汁液或生命力是否能被稱為植物中之火。
吠陀中的阿格尼

因此我們看到，雖然在希臘語和拉丁語中，赫淮斯托斯（Hephaistos）、沃爾卡努斯（Vulcanus）、赫斯提亞（Hestia）和維斯塔（Vesta）之類的名字不過是單純的名字，幾乎沒有任何普通名詞的力量或詞源學的背景，但梨俱吠陀中的阿格尼（Agni）只是火的名字，正如它在每個家庭和每個爐灶上所見到的那樣。在某些讚歌中，我們發現阿格尼被提及的方式就像普通的火一樣。我們讀到：「祭司們通過摩擦兩根火木棒產生了火（agni）。阿格尼停留在雙木中，如同在他的母親中。」「阿格尼由阿格尼點燃，」等等。但是我們在其他地方也可以看到，爐灶上的火是多麼容易地變成人性化的，甚至超越人性的東西，比如在新娘離開家時所說的言辭中，她向燃燒在爐灶上的火告別。在1526年至1530年間寫成的《蘇道爾小冊》（Sudauer Büchlein）中，該冊描述了薩姆蘭西北角，新娘在向火致辭時說：「喔，我親愛的神聖的小火啊，喔我的親愛的甜蜜的潘尼克（Oho mey mile swente panike）。」在十七世紀，立陶宛人仍將火稱為「神聖小火」（szwenta Ponyke（ugnele））。在晚上，當覆蓋它時，他們說：「神聖小火啊，我會將你埋得很好，使你不要對我生氣。」通過這類表達，可見的火被難以察覺地轉變成了某種人格化的東西，雖然還不是神聖的。

火可能被以許多不同的名字稱呼，我們在《吠陀》中可以看到，對阿耆尼（Agni）所用名字的最細微改變，如何賦予他新的主動人格，一種人性的，甚至超人的性質。他被稱為信使（I, 12, i），家族或氏族的主人（I, 12, 2）。他摧毀邪惡的靈魂（I, 12, 5），保護他的朋友（I, 12, 8），他是歌手的天佑（pramati）（I, 31, 9）。諸神使他成為人類的主宰（I, 31, 11）；他是諸神的朋友（I, 31, 1），他對其信徒如父親般（I, 31, 10）的親屬。沒有任何人，沒有任何神大於阿耆尼（I, 19, 2），他是永恆不朽的（I, 13, 5），並將人類帶向最高的永生（I, 31, 7）；他是首位的造物者（I, 13, 10），即世界的木匠（tvash^ar）。所有這一切即便對我們也是可以理解的。當祭壇上的火以煙霧上升天空的形式向諸神奉獻祭品時，788 《吠陀》中的阿耆尼。[章節

阿格尼（Agni）自然被稱為信使。當他在爐火上燃燒、在家的中心熊熊烈火時，他就是家族的主人；當他驅散黑暗、淨化空氣時，他就是邪靈的摧毀者。由於在嚴冬中若沒有火幾乎不可能維持生命，他乃是那些奉養他之人的保護者，甚至保護那些投靠他的窮人。由於沒有阿格尼就不可能進行祭祀，他因此可以被稱為諸神的友伴；又由於他被允許熄滅，或者說他總是重新復活，他被稱為不朽者。有時據說阿格尼由阿格尼而被點燃（Ⅰ，12，6），也就是說，他自己生成自己，火生火，正是在這個意義上他被稱為坦奴帕特（Tanunapat），即自身之後裔或自生者。由於他清晨的光明揭示了天地，可以說他顯現或造生了天地；一旦被構想為造物者或創造者，我們就不難理解（Ⅰ，67，3）為何他被表現為支撐大地以及透過強大的咒力維持蒼穹。這一關於阿格尼的宇宙創生特性已被塞納爾先生¹充分闡發，無須再贅言。他明確展示了晨曦如何被視為創造的原型，以及阿格尼作為光明如何每日成為世界的揭示者²或造物者，進而又如何被構想為時間初始之際的原始創造者³。因此，在 --- ¹ Senart, Legende du Buddha, p. 96.
2 Rv. IV, 3, 11：Aviṣ svaḥ abhavat gate agnau，「當阿格尼誕生時，天空被顯露。」
3 梨俱吠陀 VI, 7, 6：Vaisvanarasya vimitani mkshasa sanuni diva/e，「天之高處乃由其光而創造。」
VI] 吠陀中的阿格尼 789 太陽神一如太陽本身，被提升至至高神祇的地位，而賦予阿格尼的每一個名號、阿格尼所具現化的所有神聖性格，無論是薩維特爾（Savitṛ）、毗濕奴（Vishṇu）、布瓦（Bhuvah）、羅希塔（Rohita）、斯坎巴（Skambha）、帕傑帕蒂（Prajāpati），以及後來的普魯沙（Purusha）和梵天（Brahman），都被提升至相同的地位。

我們也必須記住，「事後即因後」（post hoc, propter hoc）的信念是許多古代神話的根源，人們便是以這種方式說服自己相信，或至少聲稱，晨間祭祀導致了阿格尼（Agni）或太陽的升起。因此我們在《梨俱吠陀》第五卷第六篇第四頌中讀到：「我們點燃你，光輝的阿格尼啊，永不朽滅的天神，使你更為榮耀的火焰在天空中閃耀」，而這一觀念在《婆羅門書》中得到了更充分的闡發。因此我們在《舍得婆羅門書》第二卷第三章第一至五節中讀到：「當他在日出前進行晨間祭祀時，他產生了那個（日子），它成為光明，升起時閃閃發光。」

經過一段時間，雅格尼（Agni）雖然保留了他的名字，卻不再僅僅是祭壇上看得見的火焰。一旦他的主要屬性——光和熱——在自然界的其他地方被認識到，雅格尼便被設想為無處不在。點燃在爐火上的火焰，人們懇求它不要熄滅，而在同一首頌詩中，人們又要求它在白晝或天空中閃耀，好讓人們永遠能看到太陽，亦即得以活著。《雅朱吠陀》十二卷第二章第18節：「點燃的雅格尼啊，未曾被喚請者，勿使他遠離我們，在此處光耀吧，在天空中光耀吧，讓我們永遠看得見太陽。」（Samiddho agna ahuta sa no mabhyapakrami/i, atraiva didihi dyavi, dyuk ha suryam drise）因此雅格尼被認知於太陽之中，人們假設他製造了太陽，無論太陽昇起或落下。《梨俱吠陀》第十卷第156章第4節：「雅格尼啊，汝已將那不褪色的星辰舉起至天空」；參見Bergaigne氏著，第一卷第141頁；Oldenberg氏著《吠陀宗教》第110頁。
790 阿耆尼在吠陀中。[章]
